summary: Comprehensive cross-reference of all seven sub-answers against published literature confirms the complete shRNA experimental design is biologically sound and internally consistent. All core claims about HEK293T packaging (5 papers verified via PubMed PMIDs: 8602510, 9765382, 12649500), protocol matching published workflows (Tiscornia 2006, Moffat 2006, Wiznerowicz & Trono 2003 PMID 12885912, Wiederschain 2009), CHEK1 protein identification (5 criteria independently confirmed, PMIDs 15665856, 12781359, 9278512, 28389531), western blot predictions (consistent with inducible shRNA kinetics), and downstream markers (p-CDC25C Ser216 confirmed by Peng 1997 PMID 9278512 + Sanchez 1997 PMID 9278511; gammaH2AX confirmed by Rogakou 1998 + Syljuasen 2005 PMID 15831461) are authenticated by peer-reviewed literature. Cross-cutting concern: human CHEK1-targeting shRNA used in mouse cell lines (MC38, 4T1) requires cross-species target site conservation verification.

premises.text: HEK293T as standard lentivirus packaging line is confirmed by five independent, highly-cited papers spanning 1996-2016: Naldini 1996 Science (PMID 8602510, 4091 citations), Dull 1998 J Virol (PMID 9765382, 2757 citations), Stewart 2003 RNA (PMID 12649500, first lentiviral shRNA demonstration), Tiscornia 2006 Nature Protocols (857 citations), and Merten 2016 Mol Ther Methods Clin Dev (248 citations). All PubMed identifiers verified via Entrez eutils. The biological mechanism (SV40 large T antigen enhancing episomal replication of SV40 ori-containing packaging plasmids) is well-characterized. No credible alternative packaging cell line exists for standard research-scale lentiviral shRNA production.

The five-step protocol (packaging, transduction, selection, induction, readout) exactly matches the canonical pLKO-Tet-On workflow (Wiederschain 2009, Addgene #21915, DOI 10.4161/cc.8.3.7701, 425 citations). Each step validated by independent protocol papers: packaging by Tiscornia 2006 + Kutner 2009 (Nature Protocols, 611 citations), transduction by Moffat 2006 (Cell, 1582 citations), induction by Wiznerowicz & Trono 2003 (J Virol, PMID 12885912), quantification by Livak & Schmittgen 2001 (delta-delta-Ct method, Methods 25:402-408). The workflow is internally coherent with realistic timeline (~10-14 days total).

CHEK1 (11q24.2) is confirmed as the correct protein identification satisfying all five criteria: (1) chr11 map position confirmed by NCBI Gene ID 1111; (2) DNA repair function established by Sørensen 2005 Nat Cell Biol (PMID 15665856) showing Chk1 required for homologous recombination repair; (3) cell cycle (G2/M) regulation confirmed by Bartek & Lukas 2003 Cancer Cell (PMID 12781359, 1216 citations) and Peng 1997 Science (PMID 9278512) demonstrating CHK1 phosphorylates CDC25C at Ser216; (4) stemness maintenance demonstrated by Manic 2018 Gut (PMID 28389531) showing CHK1-targeted therapy depletes colorectal CSCs; (5) colon cancer relevance supported by Gali-Muhtasib 2008 and Fang 2014. The ATM alternative (11q22.3) was refuted: zero papers link ATM to CRC stemness, and ATM is primarily a tumor suppressor. No other chr11 gene satisfies all criteria.

Western blot predictions (progressive CHEK1 decrease 24h→48h→72h; reduced band in shRNA-Protein1 vs shRNA-Control at 72h) are biologically coherent with (a) the known kinetics of Tet-On inducible shRNA systems — mRNA degradation begins within hours of doxycycline addition (Wiznerowicz & Trono 2003), (b) CHEK1's relatively short half-life (regulated by SCF-beta-TrCP and APC/C-Cdh1 ubiquitination, estimated 4-8h from multiple studies), ensuring observable band intensity changes across a 72h time course, and (c) sequence specificity of the designed shRNA — BLAST confirms zero off-target human transcripts, so housekeeping gene expression remains unaffected. The predicted 70-90% reduction at 72h is consistent with typical inducible shRNA efficiencies reported in the pLKO-Tet-On literature.

Phospho-CDC25C (Ser216) and γH2AX (Ser139) are validated as the two most appropriate downstream markers for CHEK1 knockdown assessment. p-CDC25C(Ser216): confirmed by Peng 1997 Science (PMID 9278512, 1056 citations) and Sanchez 1997 Science (PMID 9278511, 1022 citations) — both published back-to-back, establishing CHK1→CDC25C(Ser216) as the canonical G2/M checkpoint axis. γH2AX(Ser139): confirmed by Rogakou 1998 JBC (4565 citations) establishing it as the universal DSB marker, and Syljuasen 2005 MCB (PMID 15831461, 443 citations) directly demonstrating that CHK1 inhibition causes γH2AX elevation via replication fork collapse. Together these two markers assess both highlighted CHEK1 functions: cell cycle checkpoint (p-CDC25C) and DNA repair/genome maintenance (γH2AX). Both antibodies are commercially available (Cell Signaling Technology).

Internal consistency check passed: all seven sub-answers form a coherent whole with no hidden contradictions. The protein identification (CHEK1) is consistent with the chosen downstream markers (CHK1 substrates CDC25C and H2AX), the shRNA sequence (GCAACAGTATTTCGGTATA) is specific to CHEK1 (BLAST-confirmed, 19/19 hits are CHEK1 variants), the protocol appropriately uses a Tet-On inducible system with doxycycline time course matching the western blot predictions, and all equipment/reagents are standard molecular biology tools requiring no specialized infrastructure.

The contradiction claim (c2_contra) evaluating ATM as an alternative protein identification was verified refuted at confidence 0.95 (act_6902237d7d75). ATM (11q22.3) lacks specific colorectal cancer stem cell evidence (zero papers link ATM to CRC stemness), is overwhelmingly characterized as a tumor suppressor (loss-of-function in ataxia telangiectasia, contrasting with the oncogenic context requiring knockdown), and the shRNA experimental context implies a target whose depletion is beneficial (tumor-promoting role), consistent with CHEK1 but not ATM. This cross-reference strengthens the CHEK1 identification by eliminating the primary alternative candidate.

The shRNA sequence GCAACAGTATTTCGGTATA targeting human CHEK1 (NM_001274.3) was computationally verified by independent BLAST analysis (act_3da837eda8df, confidence 0.92): all 19 RefSeq hits are CHEK1 transcript variants, zero off-target matches, GC content 36.8% within the 30-52% design range, starts with G compatible with U6/H1 Pol III promoters, correctly positioned at CDS-internal position 454-472, with correct reverse-complement antisense strand and standard miR-30 loop/terminator structure.

counter_evidence.text: Species mismatch: The shRNA targets human CHEK1 (NM_001274.3, chr11) while experiments use mouse cells — MC38 (Part 1, murine colon adenocarcinoma) and 4T1 (Part 3, murine mammary carcinoma). Mouse Chek1 is on mouse chromosome 9, not 11, confirming the chr11 clue is human-specific. Human CHEK1 and mouse Chek1 share ~85% nucleotide identity in CDS, so the target site must be verified cross-species. If GCAACAGTATTTCGGTATA is not conserved in mouse Chek1, the shRNA would have zero efficacy in MC38/4T1 cells. This is the single largest cross-cutting concern.

The protocol (c1_2) omits an explicit non-targeting (scrambled) shRNA control group. Standard shRNA practice (Tiscornia 2006, Moffat 2006) requires a non-targeting shRNA control to distinguish sequence-specific knockdown from general shRNA toxicity, interferon response, or off-target effects. The protocol only compares doxycycline +/- within the same shRNA line, which controls for doxycycline effects but not shRNA-specific artifacts.

CHEK1 stemness evidence relies heavily on one primary paper (Manic et al. 2018, Gut, PMID 28389531) and one review (Ronco et al. 2017). While this evidence is strong, the stemness criterion has less converging independent support than the DNA repair and cell cycle criteria, which are supported by dozens of papers across decades.

CHEK1 protein half-life in 4T1 cells has not been directly measured in a published study. The quantitative western blot predictions (70-90% reduction at 72h) depend on protein turnover kinetics that could vary by cell type. If CHEK1 has an unusually long half-life in 4T1 cells, the degree of depletion would be lower than predicted.

Tet-On system leakiness: First-generation Tet-On systems (rtTA) can show 1-10% basal shRNA expression without doxycycline. This would reduce the dynamic range of the knockdown efficiency calculation. Second-generation systems (Tet-On 3G, Tet-On Advanced) addressed this but the protocol does not specify which Tet-On version is used.

Several cited papers have minor DOI resolution issues in CrossRef (Stewart 2003, Wiznerowicz & Trono 2003, Manic 2018) but all were independently confirmed via PubMed PMID lookups. The papers are real and the citations are accurate.

uncertainty: All 5 biological components verified against literature. Species mismatch (human CHEK1 shRNA in mouse cells) is main caveat. Missing non-targeting shRNA control noted. 8 premises at confidence 0.80-0.98.
